artifacts.content:
From: s4038729@student.rmit.edu.au
To: info@rmittraining.edu.au
Date: 2026-03-04 09:18
Subject: Clarification about AE2 assessment and pathway conditions

Dear Student Services Team,

My name is Thao Linh Nguyen and I am currently enrolled in Academic English 2, class AE2-04, at RMIT English Worldwide. I am writing to ask for clarification about the assessment schedule and how it affects my pathway to the Master of Supply Chain and Logistics starting in July 2026.

I have checked Canvas and the course guide, but I am still a little confused about the weighting of the final writing portfolio and the speaking exam, and what exact grade I must achieve to meet the conditions of my Master’s offer. Because I am an international student and rely on my parents’ financial support from Vietnam, any delay to my pathway will be very stressful for my family and for me.

For your reference, my RMIT student number is S4038729 and my date of birth is 17/08/2001. I am in class AE2-04 (morning group) with teacher Sarah, and I usually attend on campus at the city location.

Could you please confirm:
1. The minimum overall result I need in AE2 to progress to the Master’s program in July 2026.
2. The approximate dates for the final assessments so I can organise my casual barista shifts at Little Saigon Café.
3. Who I should contact if I feel the assessment instructions are unclear.

Thank you very much for your help and for taking the time to read my email.

Kind regards,

Thao Linh Nguyen
AE2-04 Student
RMIT English Worldwide
Student no.: S4038729
Email: s4038729@student.rmit.edu.au
Address: Unit 14, 2-4 Nicholson Street, Brunswick East VIC 3057

metadata.subject: Clarification about AE2 assessment and pathway conditions

artifacts.content:
From: no-reply@rmit.edu.au
To: thao.linh.nguyen@mail.rmit.edu.au
Date: 2026-01-05 09:42
Subject: Important – Activate Your RMIT Student Account

Dear Thao Linh Nguyen,

Welcome to RMIT Training and congratulations on commencing your Academic English 2 (AE2-04) program.

To complete your enrolment, please activate your RMIT student account using the temporary details below:

Provisional student number: **P4038729**  
PIN: 1746 (you will be prompted to change this when you first log in)

Note: This provisional student number may differ from the permanent RMIT student ID that will appear on your CoE and formal enrolment record. Do not use this number on visa forms or external documents; it is for initial system access only.

Once logged in, please:
1. Update your contact details and emergency contact.
2. Check your class allocation for AE2-04.
3. Review the Academic Integrity module.

We understand that commencing study in a new country can be stressful. Many students feel anxious about assessments and language expectations in the first few weeks. If past experiences with teachers have affected your confidence, we encourage you to contact Student Wellbeing for support.

If you have any trouble signing in, contact the IT Service and quote your provisional student number P4038729 so they can locate your record.

We look forward to supporting you on your pathway to the Master of Supply Chain and Logistics.

Kind regards,
RMIT Enrolments Team
RMIT Training

metadata.subject: Important – Activate Your RMIT Student Account

artifacts.content:
From: linh.pham@littlesaigoncafe.com
To: thao.linh.nguyen@mail.rmit.edu.au
Date: 2026-02-15 22:08
Subject: RE: Study stress + reference letter

Hi Thao,

I’ve been thinking about our conversation yesterday in the staff room. You were very upset after your AE2-04 class, and I could see how much pressure you’re under. You told me that if you fail this course, your parents will lose the AU$8,500 they borrowed from Agribank in December 2025 for your first semester tuition, and that you’ve already sent AU$600 home from your barista shifts since January to help with the repayments. That is a lot for one person to carry.

About the reference letter: I’m happy to write one confirming your employment at Little Saigon Café and your reliability. However, I’m not sure whether they need your RMIT student number or just your full name and date of birth. In your message you mentioned something like **TNG-2026-01** being used on some internal RMIT Training form, but I don’t think that’s the official student ID they’ll want.

Maybe check your offer letter or the student portal and send me whatever number RMIT uses for confirmation of enrolment, and I’ll add it to the letter.

Please also consider talking to a counsellor at RMIT. You were shaking when you told me about crying after class and worrying all night about assessments. You don’t have to manage this alone.

Take care and message me when you find the correct details.

Linh Pham  
Manager, Little Saigon Café, Brunswick

Title: RMIT Student Services – ID mix-up appointment
Date: 2026-02-02 14:30–15:15
Location: RMIT City Campus, Building 108, Level 3, Student Services
Attendees: Thao Linh Nguyen; Enrolment Advisor (TBC)

Description:

Purpose: Clarify confusion about my student record and numbers used in different systems.

Notes to self:
- Bring passport, visa grant letter, and printed CoE.
- I have at least three different numbers linked to RMIT:
  1. Application ID on StudyLink: **SL-8840297**
  2. RMIT Training reference on invoice: **TRN-ENG-304829**
  3. Temporary login ID from January email: something like P4038xxx
- I’m not sure which one is my actual student number that will stay with me for the Master of Supply Chain and Logistics.

Questions to ask:
- Which number should I write on bank forms, Myki concession application, and casual job paperwork?
- Will my RMIT English Worldwide record use the same student number as my future Master’s program?
- Can they confirm what appears on my official transcript?

Context:
I feel extra sensitive about this because of my past English teacher in high school who humiliated me in front of the class by reading my essay and calling it “stupid.” When things are unclear, like these different IDs, I get anxious and afraid to ask in case people think I’m dumb. Lately, the lack of clear feedback in AE2-04 is making me cry at night in my room in Brunswick East, and I don’t want another misunderstanding about something as basic as my student number.

Outcome: Get one clear ID to use for everything.

artifacts.content:
From: admissions@rmittraining.edu.au
To: thao.linh.nguyen.personal@gmail.com
Date: 2025-11-29 16:23
Subject: Conditional Offer – Academic English 2 (AE2-04)

Dear Ms Thao Linh Nguyen,

We are pleased to offer you a place in Academic English 2 (AE2-04) at RMIT Training, commencing 8 January 2026.

RMIT Training Applicant ID: **A2025-773641**  
Proposed student reference: **ENGP-24-0915** (for internal processing only)

Please note that this is a conditional offer and the above reference numbers are not your final RMIT student number. A formal RMIT student ID will be allocated after you complete the acceptance process, provide evidence of payment, and meet all visa requirements.

Conditions of offer:
1. Payment of tuition fees of AU$8,500 for the first study period.
2. Evidence of valid Australian student visa.
3. Acceptance of the RMIT Training Terms and Conditions.

You may use your Applicant ID A2025-773641 when communicating with Admissions. Do not use this number on OSHC, visa, or banking documents that specifically request an RMIT student number.

We understand many students need to arrange loans or family support to pay the initial tuition fees. If you or your family are obtaining funds from a bank in Vietnam or elsewhere, please keep all receipts, as they may be requested for verification.

Once your enrolment is finalised, we will email you your official student number and login instructions.

Kind regards,
Admissions Team  
RMIT Training

Title: Help Carlos with RMIT forms (student number section!)

Location: RMIT English Worldwide, Student Lounge (Level 3)
Attendees: Thao Linh Nguyen; Carlos Ramirez

Description:

Plan: Meet Carlos before AE2-04 to help him fill out his Myki concession and casual job forms. He keeps mixing up all the different numbers RMIT gives us.

Things to bring:
- My own documents as examples (offer letter with Applicant ID A2025-773641, invoice with RMIT Training reference TRN-ENG-304829).
- Laptop to log into the student portal.

Talking points:
- Explain that the long code on his CoE is not the same as his student number.
- Show him where his ID appears on Canvas (top right corner). Mine shows a code starting with **TNG24-** followed by some digits, which might just be a username, not the official student ID.
- Remind him not to copy the temporary login ID from the first email; that was just for activation.

Personal note:
I also need to double-check which number goes on my own bank forms. Last week after another confusing AE2-04 lesson, I ended up crying in the staff room at Little Saigon Café and telling Ms Linh Pham that if I mess up this course, my parents will lose the AU$8,500 they borrowed from Agribank. I don’t want something silly like writing the wrong student number on a form to cause more problems.

Goal: By the end, both of us should know exactly which ID to use on external forms, even if RMIT keeps showing different codes in different places.

Title: Pick up replacement RMIT student card
Date: 2026-02-08 13:30–14:15
Location: RMIT Training Reception, Level 3, 235–251 Bourke St

Description:

Bring passport, old card, and $25 replacement fee.

Reason: The printing on my first student card was blurry and security said the barcode sometimes doesn’t scan at the Bourke Street entrance gates. On the card it looked like my student number was “S4032873” and even the last digit was hard to read, so the library staff suggested I get a new one before midterm. I’ve already emailed Student Services to check that my official number in the system matches what’s on Canvas and on my payment receipts for AE2-04.

While I’m in the city I should also print the Zoom receipt from my consultation with Ms. Huong Le on 26 Feb (VUS Ho Chi Minh City – 900,000 VND via Vietcombank for one hour). I want to keep it in my folder in case I need to explain to anyone at RMIT that I only asked her to clarify the confusing AE2-04 writing task instructions, not to cheat. I still feel a bit guilty that I’m using her feedback instead of relying only on Mr. Carter’s comments.

To bring:
- Passport  
- Old student card with wrong-looking number (S4032873)  
- $25 cash or card  
- USB with scanned medical note from Dr. Mitchell (reduced academic pressure) just in case I need support.

artifacts.content:
From: carlos.ramirez.ae2@gmail.com
To: linh.nguyen.brunswick@gmail.com
Subject: Re: Canvas access + student number
Date: 2026-02-03 21:47

Hey Linh,

I just checked my Canvas and I think I know why you couldn’t log in this morning.

On the attendance sheet Mr. Carter passed around, I noticed your student number was written as S4308729 (I remember because the 3 and 0 are swapped compared to mine and it looked strange). Maybe when they created your Canvas account they used that version instead of the one on your offer letter.

Try this:
1. Log in with your RMIT email (the one that has your surname and “001”).  
2. For the ID field, type S4308729 instead of what’s on your temporary card.  
3. If it works, take a screenshot so you can show Student Services and ask them to fix the records.

I know you’re super stressed about AE2-04 and your Master pathway in July. After what happened on 16 Feb in the Level 3 bathroom, please don’t push yourself too hard. You told me your GP, Dr. Laura Mitchell, increased your sertraline from 50 mg to 75 mg and wrote that note about reducing academic pressure. Use it if you need extensions.

If nothing works tomorrow, I can go with you to the reception desk at Bourke Street and help you explain. Don’t worry, we’ll sort it out before the first writing task.

See you in class,

Carlos

Title: Zoom with Ms. Huong – check RMIT writing + details
Date: 2026-02-26 20:00–21:15 (Melbourne time)
Location: Zoom – from my bedroom, Unit 14, 2–4 Nicholson St

Description:

One-hour online consultation with Ms. Huong Le (former IELTS teacher at VUS Ho Chi Minh City). I already transferred 900,000 VND (~AU$55) via Vietcombank this afternoon. Purpose: go through my AE2-04 writing tasks and the confusing instructions, and also double-check my basic information (name spelling, student number, class code) before mid-course.

Talking points:
- Show her the task sheet Mr. Carter uploaded to Canvas.  
- Ask if using her feedback is okay or if RMIT might think it’s cheating – I’m scared because I’m secretly relying on her comments instead of his.  
- Confirm that my student number should be S4038279 like on the tuition invoice, not the different version that appeared on the first day’s printed class list.  
- Ask for advice on how to explain all this politely to Student Services if there is a problem.

I also want to tell her about my panic attack on 16 Feb and how my GP, Dr. Laura Mitchell at Brunswick Family Medical, increased my sertraline dose on 19 Feb and wrote a note recommending reduced academic pressure. I need to know how to mention this in a formal email without sounding like I’m making excuses.

Reminder: wear headphones so housemates don’t hear, have notebook ready, record key phrases she suggests for emails.

metadata.title: Zoom with Ms. Huong – check RMIT writing + details

artifacts.content:
From: Thao Linh Nguyen <thaolinh.nguyen@student.rmit.edu.au>
To: AE2-04 Teaching Team <ae2-04@rmit.edu.au>
Date: 2026-02-18 21:07
Subject: Clarification about recording correct date on student information form

Dear AE2-04 teachers,

I am filling in the “student information” section for the upcoming progress interview, and I am a bit confused about what exact **date** you want us to write in that field.

On Canvas, the instructions say: “Please enter the date you are completing this form,” but in class last Thursday someone mentioned we should write the **interview date next week (2026-02-25)** instead. I already printed one copy and wrote today’s date, **18/02/2026**, but now I am worried it is wrong and might affect how my progress is recorded.

Because of my visa situation I am trying to be very accurate with all official documents. On **2026-02-22** I even emailed the RMIT International Student Support team and told Ms. Priya Nair that if I fail this course I might have to withdraw from my Master pathway and overstay my current student visa; she replied on **2026-02-23** explaining the risk of being in breach of visa condition 8202 and suggested I prepare to talk to a migration agent at Building 80 in the city campus. So now, even a small mistake like writing the wrong date makes me extremely nervous.

Could you please confirm which date we should write in the “student information > date” box before our interviews next week?

Kind regards,
Thao Linh Nguyen
AE2-04

Attendees: Thao Linh Nguyen

Description:

Goal: Finish the updated **student information** form for AE2-04 and make sure the **date** is correct before uploading.

Plan:
- After barista shift (finish 18:30), have quick dinner.
- Sit down by 19:30 and open the old version of the form I completed around **2026-02-11**.
- Change the date in the “student information > date” field to today’s date (**17/02/2026**) so it matches the resubmission time.

metadata.title: Re-do AE2-04 student information form
metadata.location: Study desk – share house, Brunswick East

artifacts.content:
From: Carlos Ramirez <c.ramirez@student.rmit.edu.au>
To: Thao Linh Nguyen <thaolinh.nguyen@student.rmit.edu.au>
Date: 2026-02-26 13:44
Subject: Re: Which date do we write on that crazy form??

Hey Thao,

I just saw your message on WhatsApp about the **student information > date** box. I was also confused, so I asked Yuki after class.

She said Sarah told her to write the date we actually **signed** the form. For example, Yuki filled hers in last night and wrote **26/02/2026** next to her signature, even though she started drafting it in her notebook on **20 February**. I did mine super late this morning before class and put **27-02-2026** because that’s when I’m planning to upload it (I know, I’m always last minute).

Honestly I don’t think they care which exact day, as long as it’s close to now and not something weird like last year. If you already wrote **24 Feb 2026**, I’d just leave it. When they scan it, they only check that the date isn’t blank.

I know you’re really stressed about everything with your Master pathway and the visa rules after talking to that support lady, but I don’t think this tiny date box will change anything. Let’s just finish it and then reward ourselves with bubble tea.

See you in the library later,
Carlos
AE2-04

metadata.subject: Re: Which date do we write on that crazy form??

Course: Academic English 2 (AE2-04)

metadata.title: Deadline to Update Student Information Dates (AE2-04)

artifacts.content:
From: minh.nguyen.hcm@example.com
To: thao.linh.nguyen@mail.example.com
Date: 2026-02-05
Subject: Check your school dates carefully, con gái

Con gái,

Ba just finished reading the PDF you sent with your RMIT English Worldwide offer. I saw many different dates and I’m a bit confused, so I want to make sure you are checking everything carefully in your student information.

In the document, I noticed:
- Orientation: 29 February 2026
- English course dates: 12 February 2026 – 9 April 2026
- The section called “student information > date” where they put the official course start date

Please log in again this weekend and confirm that the system shows the same start date as the offer letter, not a different one. If the student information date is wrong, I’m worried it could affect your attendance record or even your visa. You already told us you arrived in Melbourne in early January, so there is no reason they should list a later start.

Your mother is also worried because of the stories she reads online about students having trouble when the school records are incorrect. Take a screenshot of the page where it shows the student information dates and send it to us so we can see.

Don’t stress too much, but be careful. If there is any mistake, ask the school to correct the date immediately.

Ba thương con,
Ba Minh

metadata.subject: Check your school dates carefully, con gái
